stimulus:
The following text is adapted from Sarah Orne Jewett’s 1894 sketch “A Lost Lover.”

One soft September afternoon, in the course of a walking ramble that I had begun without much plan among the back roads of the central counties of Maine, I became, with no little vexation, uncertain about the lane I was following. The hills had risen and fallen by such close turns; and the path, for the last half hour, had crossed and recrossed itself between the orchards and the stone walls so confusingly, that I no longer had any clear notion in which direction lay the pleasant village of P——, where I had intended to take my lodging for the night.
stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: The narrator recalls fond memories of an afternoon spent walking among Maine’s scenic hills.
The narrator describes the sights he encountered during a long walk through a region he often visits.
The narrator explains the difficulties he faced during a walking ramble and how he overcame them.
The narrator recounts a walking ramble he undertook and admits to becoming lost.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The narrator is “vexed” about the lane and concedes that he no longer has any clear notion of the direction to P——, his intended lodging — that is, he is recounting the ramble and admitting that he became lost.

Choice A is incorrect because the narrator describes vexation rather than fondness and does not dwell on scenic beauty. Choice B is incorrect because the text does not indicate the region is one he often visits. Choice C is incorrect because the excerpt ends with him still lost — he does not explain how he overcame the difficulty.
